Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: The company operated as a major oil trading entity in Singapore under the direction of its founding family [cite: 79-80].
The company was placed under interim judicial management by a court order, which transferred the management of its affairs, business, and property to independent officeholders[cite: 84, 212].
The governing court order authorized the interim judicial managers to exercise all powers and entitlements of directors conferred by the Companies Act and the company's constitution[cite: 213].
The appointed interim judicial managers exercised their management powers to retain a specific law firm to provide legal services to the company[cite: 91].
The company's directors, despite being divested of operational control, formally objected to the choice of legal counsel selected by the interim judicial managers[cite: 68, 98].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, following the appointment of interim judicial managers who possess the express power to manage a company's affairs, directors retain a residual common law entitlement to initiate litigation in the company's name to challenge management decisions [cite: 69-70, 126].
Party_Details.Rule: Upon the making of an insolvency management order, the directors are effectively divested of their powers of management. Such powers, including the exclusive right to bring or defend legal proceedings in the company's name, are transferred to the court-appointed officeholders[cite: 170, 217, 260].
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff (acting through directors) stated that directors retain residual management powers to protect the company's interests, including the power to restrain solicitors. The Defendant stated that the statutory regime and the specific court orders transferred all litigation-related authority to the interim judicial managers to the exclusion of the directors[cite: 113, 275, 516].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the appointment of interim judicial managers renders the board of directors functus officio regarding the company's management. Directors retain only extremely narrow residual powers to challenge the insolvency order itself. Seeking to injunct the law firm chosen by the managers does not challenge the manager's appointment but rather interferes with their operational management. Consequently, the directors had no authority to sue in the company's name[cite: 227, 389, 534, 552].

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP

Facts.Text: The respondent is a limited liability partnership providing legal services in Singapore[cite: 8].
The firm was formally retained by the interim judicial managers of an oil trading company to advise and act for the company and its managers[cite: 13, 91].
The firm acted for the company under the direction of the insolvency officeholders, who had been granted all directorial powers by the High Court[cite: 213, 273].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a law firm validly retained by a company's interim judicial managers can be restrained from acting by the company's directors who have been divested of their management powers[cite: 68, 519].
Party_Details.Rule: A company under judicial management must act through its appointed managers. If the managers choose to retain or continue a set of solicitors, that choice is a management function within their sole prerogative[cite: 262, 524].
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant stated it was properly instructed by the only individuals with the legal authority to represent the company. The Plaintiff stated that the firm's prior involvement created a conflict that justified directorial intervention[cite: 520, 525].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal found that the law firm was retained by the only parties possessing the authority to manage the company. The directors' attempt to 'restrain' the solicitors was an attempt to forcibly bring about a state of affairs that the managers did not sanction. The firm was not improperly acting, and the attempts to injunct them were taken without authority[cite: 525, 531, 554].

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd (under Interim Judicial Management)

Facts.Text: The company was a related entity to Hin Leong Trading and operated in the shipping and tanker industry[cite: 80].
A High Court order placed the company under interim judicial management, divesting directors of their power to manage the business and property of the entity[cite: 85, 90, 212].
The interim judicial managers, exercising their court-granted entitlements, continued the retainer of a law firm to manage the company's legal affairs[cite: 91, 273].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether directors of a shipping company retain the standing to initiate corporate legal actions after a court has transferred all management and directorial powers to interim judicial managers[cite: 125, 151].
Party_Details.Rule: The appointment of a judicial manager entails a substitution and supplantation of the powers ordinarily vested in the board of directors. This excludes directors from conducting litigation or discharging company solicitors[cite: 290, 424, 523].
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff (acting through former management) stated they retained residual authority to dictate the company's legal representation. The Defendant stated that the directors were functus officio and had no legal standing to represent the company[cite: 287, 513].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court confirmed that the statutory scheme vests authority over the company's affairs in the managers to the exclusion of the directors. The directors' attempt to start litigation without the managers' consent was unauthorized. The court upheld the striking out of the actions[cite: 516, 552, 554].

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP

Facts.Text: The firm acted as legal counsel for the shipping company under the express authorization of the court-appointed interim judicial managers[cite: 91, 97].
The firm's authority to act was challenged by directors who had been removed from the management of the company's affairs by the judicial management regime[cite: 80, 525].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a law firm is subject to a restraining order initiated by directors when the firm's client—the company—is under the exclusive control of judicial managers who support the retainer[cite: 520, 524].
Party_Details.Rule: Management power includes the power to choose who represents the company. Directors cannot use the court's machinery to override the decisions of managers in whom those powers are currently vested[cite: 170, 517, 525].
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant stated that the directors' actions were a non-starter because they lacked the requisite authority to manage the company's litigation. The Plaintiff stated that residual powers allowed them to interfere with the retainer[cite: 275, 420].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the appeal, noting that recognizing the directors' purported power would outflank the purpose of the judicial management scheme. The firm's retainer by the managers was valid, and the directors had no authority to terminate it or sue in the company's name to achieve that end[cite: 516, 534, 602].
